topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with irritating persistence. Sarah stared at the spreadsheet on her screen, her jaw tightening almost imperceptibly. The numbers swam before her eyes: projections, salary bands, and the stark, undeniable difference between her own compensation and that of Mark, the junior analyst. She tapped a pen against the desk, the small click echoing too loudly in the quiet cubicle. *This is ridiculous,* she thought, a slow, simmering heat rising in her chest. She glanced over at Mark, who was currently laughing with a coworker, his easy confidence a sharp contrast to her own knot of frustration. When he finally turned his attention to her, offering a bright, slightly too-eager smile, Sarah managed a tight nod, her expression carefully neutral, though her fingers were gripping the edge of the desk until her knuckles were white.

[story 2]

"Just... look at this," David said, pushing the printout across the shared monitor. It was a comparison chart, and the disparity in earnings was glaringly obvious. Liam, the junior team member, looked up, a flicker of something unreadable crossing his face before he quickly masked it. David didn't elaborate, simply letting the silence stretch, heavy and awkward. David felt a distinct pressure building behind his sternum, a tight, unwelcome sensation. He cleared his throat, the sound rougher than intended. "Right. Interesting data." He forced a professional tone into his voice, though his gaze kept flicking back to Liam, cataloging the subtle shift in his posture. He wanted to say something sharp, something to push back, but the words caught in his throat, leaving only a dry, internal churning.

[story 3]

I reread the performance review notes for the third time, the ink blurring slightly under my intense focus. The figures were irrefutable. Mark was earning nearly double what I was making for the exact same output. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, a puff of air that seemed to carry all the pent-up tension in my shoulders. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and took a long, slow sip, letting the bitterness settle. When Mark walked by, exchanging a casual greeting with someone from accounting, I made sure my expression remained perfectly placid, a mask stretched taut over a rising tide of irritation. I wanted to shake something loose, to make the imbalance visible, but I kept my hands steady, focusing instead on the precise, controlled movement of lifting the mug.
